Mouth (faux), the upper or opening part of the tube in a one-petaled corolla.

Mules, see HYBRID.

N.

Naked, without leaves or hairs.

Nectar, or honey-cup, the part of the flower which secretes the honey.

Nut, a seed covered by a hard woody shell.

O.

Oblong-egg-shaped, oblong at the base, but egg-shaped towards the end.

Oligandria, eight-stamened.

Oligogynia, eight pistils.

P.

Paleaceous, chaffy.

Panicle, an irregular assemblage of flowers.

Papilionaceous flowers, butterfly-shaped, as the pea.

Parasitical plants, which grow not on the earth, but on other plants.

Pedicle, a little fruitstalk.

Pentagon, five-cornered.

Pentagynia, five pistils.

S f 2

Pentandria,

page 338 · 192174570.3.jpg
324
B O T A N Y.
History.

Pentandrio, five-stamined.
Perennial, continuing more than two years.
Petals (petala), the leaves which constitute the corolla or blossom.
Pillar (stipes), the pedicle of the down of some seeds, as in dandelion.
Pimpled (papillosus), beset with little hard protuberances.
Pistil, a part of a flower consisting of the germen, the style, and the summit.
Pitcher-shaped (urceolatus), bellying like a jug.
Pith, a soft substance filling the cavity of some plants.
Plaited, folded.
Pod (siliqua), a seed-vessel of two valves, within which the seeds are alternately fixed to each seam.
Pollen (farina), a fine powder in the anthers of flowers.
Polyadelphia, stamens in three or more sets.
Polyandria, many stamens.
Polygamia, see Introduction to the 23d class.
Pores, little holes.
Pouch, a short pod.
Prism-shaped, different from cylindrical, in having the circumference angular.
Proliferous, when one flower, &c. springs out of another.
Pubescent, clothed with soft wool or hair.
R.
Radiate, compound flowers in which the florets of the centre differ from those of the circumference. See Dijk.
Rays (radii), outer florets of a radiate compound flower. See Dijk.
Receptacle, the seat or base of a flower.
Reflected, bent back.
Remote whorls, having a considerable length of stem between each.
Rhomboidal, nearly diamond-shaped.
Rigid, inflexible.
Root-leaves (radicalia), those which proceed immediately from the root.
Ruffle or ring, the part of the curtain of an agaric which adheres to the stem after the outer part is gone.
Runner (flagellum), a barren twig lying on the ground.
S.
Salver-shaped (hypocrateriformis), when a one-petaled corolla has its lower part tubular, and its higher part flat and expanded.
Scaled (squamosus), like the skin of a fish.
Scarify (squarrosus), applied to the rough cup of some compound flowers.
Seed, that which contains the rudiments of the new plant.
Seed-lobes (cotyledones), the perishable part of a seed which affords food to the rest in germination.

Seminal leaves, those which rise from the seed-lobes.
Serrated, like the teeth of a saw.
Sheath (spatha), a kind of calyx like that of the crocus.
Simple, undivided.
Sitting leaves or flowers, without leaf or flower-stalks.
Solitary, only one in a place.
Spear-egg-shaped, like a spear at the base, and an egg at the extremity.
Spike (spica), a collection of flowers placed alternately on each side of a common fruitstalk without little fruitstalks.
Stamen, consists of a filament and anther.
Staminiferous, bearing stamens.
Streaked, marked with depressed lines.
Syngensia, united anthers.
T.
Target-shaped leaf, having the stalk fixed, not in the edge, but the centre.
Tetragynia, four pistils.
Tetradynamia, four stamens longer.
Tiled, one leaf or scale partly covering another.
Tooth-ferrated, when the edge of a leaf is marked with little serrated teeth.
Triandria, three stamens.
Trigynia, three pistils.
Tubercled, having solid warts; applied to certain lichens.
Tuberous root, having many roundish knobs in a bundle.
Tubular, hollow like a tube.
U.
Umbel (umbella), an assemblage of flowers in which a number of slender fruitstalks, proceeding from the same centre, rise to nearly the same height, so as to form a regular surface at the top, as in hemlock or carrot.
Unequal florets (radiati), when the florets of an umbel are larger in the circumference than in the centre.
Valve, the pieces of a capsule are called valves.
Vaulted (fornicatus), like the roof of the mouth.
Veil (calyptra), the calyx of mosses.
W.
Wheel-shaped, a corolla of one petal, with a flat border and a short tube.
Whirls of branches, leaves or flowers; ex gr. the branches of the fir.
Wings, the lateral petals of a butterfly-shaped blossom.
Winged leaf-stalk, with a thin membrane on each side.
— leaf, when an undivided leaf-stalk has many little leaves growing from each side.
Wing cleft, a leaf deeply cut.
Woody, opposed to herbaceous.
Wool, curly haired clothing on some plants.
Z.
Zigzag, having many contrary bendings.

INDEX.
page 339 · 192174583.3.jpg

Zingiber Zernumbet.
Ginger. page 70.

Botanical illustration of Zingiber Zernumbet (Ginger), showing a large, ribbed rhizome with two flowers emerging from the top.

BOTANY.
Monandria.

flower.

Botanical illustration of a single flower of Zingiber Zernumbet, showing its structure and a long, narrow style.

Boerhaavia scandens.
p. 77.

Botanical illustration of Boerhaavia scandens, showing a climbing vine with opposite, heart-shaped leaves and small flowers.

Plate XCIX.

Salicornia herbacea.
Jointed Gipswort. p. 77.

Botanical illustration of Salicornia herbacea, showing a tall, jointed stem with small, opposite leaves and a terminal spike of flowers.

Diandria.

Calceolaria Nana.
p. 89.

Botanical illustration of Calceolaria Nana, showing a small plant with two tubular, bell-shaped flowers hanging from a stem with opposite leaves.

Chionanthus Zeylanica.
Orange tree. p. 83.

Botanical illustration of Chionanthus Zeylanica, showing a branch with large, opposite, ovate leaves and a terminal raceme of small flowers.

Crantbemum parvifolium.
p. 83.

Botanical illustration of Crantbemum parvifolium, showing a long, slender raceme of small flowers and a smaller, more compact raceme.

Aucubum Lucidum.
p. 93.

Botanical illustration of Aucubum Lucidum, showing a plant with a dense, branched root system and opposite, serrated leaves.

Piper Nigrum.
Black Pepper p. 93.

Botanical illustration of Piper Nigrum, showing a branch with opposite, ovate leaves and a long, drooping raceme of small berries.

Abbott Prin. Nat. Sculptor, fecit.

BOTANY.
Triandria.

Plate C.

Cyperus Papyrus p. 101.

Wibrenia Maura p. 100.

Botanical illustration of three plants: Cyperus Papyrus, Wibrenia Maura, and Maspinia Sylvatica.A detailed botanical plate featuring three distinct plant species. On the left, Cyperus Papyrus is shown with its characteristic papyrus-like leaves and a large, feathery spikelet. In the center, Maspinia Sylvatica is depicted with a single, slender stem and a small, four-leaf whorl. On the right, Wibrenia Maura is illustrated with a cluster of narrow, linear leaves and a long, slender spikelet. The plants are shown with their root systems at the bottom, emerging from a common base. The illustration is rendered in a fine-line engraving style.

Maspinia Sylvatica.
p. 101.

Abbot's Tract of Botany p. 101.

To the left, there is a smaller plant with a fan-like arrangement of leaves. Below the central tree, there are several smaller plants, including one with a cluster of upright, pointed leaves and another with a more rounded, bushy form. The entire scene is rendered in a light, monochromatic style, possibly using pencil or light washes, and is framed by a thin border.A faint botanical illustration on aged paper, showing a central tree-like plant with a large, rounded, mossy or leafy canopy. To the left, there is a smaller plant with a fan-like arrangement of leaves. Below the central tree, there are several smaller plants, including one with a cluster of upright, pointed leaves and another with a more rounded, bushy form. The entire scene is rendered in a light, monochromatic style, possibly using pencil or light washes, and is framed by a thin border.
page 343 · 192174635.3.jpg

Ryegrass fulgo.

Purple Tussock Grass.

Soft Tussock Grass.

BOTANY. Triandra.

Bulbous Foxtail Grass.

Crooping Meadow Grass.

Plate CL.

Great Meadow Grass.

Time Bent Grass.

Crooping soft Grass.

Silver Hair Grass.

Temple Grass.

Crested Grass.

Droptail Grass.

Mijra.

Feather Grass.

Meadow Grass.

Foxtail Grass.

Mountain Hair Grass.

Annual Meadow Grass.

Water Spear Grass.

page 344 · 192174648.3.jpg
Botanical illustration plate showing various plant specimens, including roots, stems, and leaves, arranged in a grid-like pattern.This is a botanical illustration plate, likely from a scientific work. It features a collection of plant specimens, possibly roots, stems, and leaves, arranged in a grid-like pattern. The specimens are rendered in a light, monochromatic style, possibly using pencil or light ink. The plants vary in size and shape, with some showing dense clusters of roots or leaves, and others showing more elongated, slender structures. The background is a plain, light-colored surface, and the overall appearance is that of an old, well-preserved scientific illustration.
page 345 · 192174661.3.jpg

BOTANY.
Tetrandria.

Plate CII.

Pretea argentea. p. 113.

Botanical illustration of Pretea argentea, showing a branch with opposite, serrated leaves and a single flower with a prominent, textured, spherical center.A detailed botanical engraving of a branch from the Pretea argentea. The branch features several pairs of opposite, ovate leaves with finely serrated margins. At the top of the branch, there is a single flower with a large, rounded, and highly textured center, possibly representing the ovary or a cluster of stamens.

Samara Lota. p. 110.

Botanical illustration of Samara Lota, showing a branch with opposite, ovate leaves and a long, cylindrical, spiky fruit or seed pod.An engraving of a branch from the Samara Lota. The branch has several pairs of opposite, ovate leaves with serrated edges. Attached to the branch is a long, cylindrical, and densely covered in small spines or bristles, which is likely a developing fruit or seed pod.

Banksia serrata. p. 113.

Botanical illustration of Banksia serrata, showing a large, round, spiky fruit or seed pod with several seeds or fruits inside.An engraving of a large, spherical fruit or seed pod from the Banksia serrata. The outer surface is covered in a dense layer of sharp, pointed spines. Inside the pod, several large, oval-shaped seeds or fruits are visible, each with a textured surface.

Dorstenia Contrayerva. p. 110.

Botanical illustration of Dorstenia Contrayerva, showing a plant with deeply lobed leaves and several star-shaped flowers or seed pods.An engraving of a plant from the Dorstenia Contrayerva. The plant has a cluster of deeply lobed, palmately divided leaves at its base. Several star-shaped structures, likely flowers or seed pods, are shown on long, thin stalks rising from the center of the plant. One of these structures is shown in detail, revealing a pattern of small dots or seeds.

Abel Prim. Wal. sculptor fecit.

A single, bright yellow flower is shown in full bloom, positioned centrally on the stem. The illustration is rendered in a light, muted greenish-yellow color.
Botanical illustration of a plant with pinnate leaves and a cluster of small flowers.A botanical illustration of a plant with pinnate leaves. The leaves are arranged in pairs along a central stem. A cluster of small, light-colored flowers is shown, positioned centrally on the stem. The illustration is rendered in a light, muted greenish-yellow color.
Botanical illustration of a plant with large, lobed leaves and a central stem.A botanical illustration of a plant with large, lobed leaves. The leaves are arranged in pairs along a central stem. The illustration is rendered in a light, muted greenish-yellow color.

Pentandria.

Plate III.

Cinchona Officinalis

Jesuits Bark. p. 133.

Botanical illustration of Cinchona Officinalis (Jesuits Bark). The central figure shows a woody stem with several large, ovate, serrated leaves and a terminal raceme of small, tubular flowers. Surrounding the main plant are detailed drawings of its reproductive parts: a single flower bud at the top left, a single flower bud at the bottom left, a single flower bud at the top right, a single flower bud at the bottom right, a single flower bud at the top left, a single flower bud at the bottom left, a single flower bud at the top right, and a single flower bud at the bottom right.

p. 130.

Cornus
Monspeliensis.
p. 137.

Botanical illustration of Tripenunia Lappenica. The main figure shows a cluster of small, succulent-like leaves at the base of a stem, with several small flowers on long, slender stalks. Below the main figure is a detailed drawing of a single flower bud.
Botanical illustration of Cornus Monspeliensis. The main figure shows a branch with several long, narrow, serrated leaves and a terminal raceme of small flowers. Below the main figure is a detailed drawing of a single flower bud.

Abbott, Prin. Wal. & sculptor fecit.

page 348 · 192174700.3.jpg
A faint botanical illustration of a plant with large, ovate leaves and a terminal inflorescence, possibly a species of Eucalyptus, on aged paper.This is a botanical illustration, likely a woodcut or engraving, rendered in a very light, muted greenish-grey ink on aged, yellowish paper. The central subject is a plant branch with several large, ovate leaves that have prominent veins. At the top of the branch is a terminal inflorescence, which appears to be a spike or raceme of small flowers or buds. To the left of the main branch, there is a smaller, more delicate plant with feathery, needle-like leaves. To the right, there are several small, rounded structures, possibly fruits or seed pods. The paper shows signs of age, including a vertical crease on the right side and some minor foxing or staining.
page 349 · 192174713.3.jpg

BOTANY.
Hexandria.

Plate CIV.

Oryza Sativa.
Rice plant. p. 160.

Tacca pinnatifida.
p. 159.

Botanical illustration of Tacca pinnatifida (Palm-leaf Amaranth).A detailed botanical illustration of Tacca pinnatifida. The main drawing shows a tall, slender stem with a cluster of long, narrow, pinnately divided leaves at the top. The leaves are deeply lobed and have a wavy, almost drooping appearance. At the base of the stem, there is a small, fibrous root system. To the right of the main plant, there is a separate, smaller illustration of a single, long, narrow leaf, showing its structure and the way it curves slightly at the tip.
Botanical illustration of Oryza Sativa (Rice).A detailed botanical illustration of Oryza Sativa. It shows a cluster of rice panicles (ears) attached to a central stem. Each panicle is composed of numerous individual spikelets, which are small, oval-shaped grains. The spikelets are arranged in a dense, branched structure. The entire cluster is supported by a network of fine, root-like fibers.

Trillium sesile.
Three leaved Night Shade p. 161.

Botanical illustration of Trillium sesile (Three-leaved Nightshade).A detailed botanical illustration of Trillium sesile. It shows a single, upright stem with a cluster of three large, ovate leaves at the top. The leaves have prominent veins and a slightly serrated margin. At the base of the stem, there is a small, fibrous root system. The illustration is rendered in a fine-line style, showing the texture of the leaves and the structure of the stem.

W. Bell, Prin. Watercolour, 1804

page 350 · 192174726.3.jpg
Botanical illustration of three plants with yellow flowers and green leaves.A botanical illustration featuring three distinct plant specimens. The top-left plant has a dense, upright cluster of small, yellow flowers. The bottom-left plant has a single, large, trifoliate leaf with a long petiole and a small yellow flower. The rightmost plant is a taller, more complex specimen with multiple long, narrow, lanceolate leaves and a cluster of yellow flowers at its apex. The background is a light, aged paper with some faint, illegible text and small brown spots.

heptandria.

Linandra pnestrata.
p. 162.

Plate CV.

Tricentalis Europaea.
Chickweed winter green, p. 162.

Botanical illustration of Tricentalis Europaea (Chickweed winter green). The drawing shows a single, upright stem with a terminal flower. The leaves are arranged in opposite pairs along the stem, with a basal rosette of leaves and a fibrous root system at the base.
Botanical illustration of Linandra pnestrata. The drawing shows a curved stem with several large, rounded, lobed leaves. Small flowers are clustered along the stem, and a few buds are visible at the tips of the branches.

Petiveria alliacea.
Guinea Henweed, p. 162.

Botanical illustration of Petiveria alliacea (Guinea Henweed). The drawing shows a slender stem with opposite, ovate leaves. Small flowers are arranged in a terminal raceme at the top of the stem.

Saururus Cernuus.
Lizards tail, p. 162.

Botanical illustration of Saururus Cernuus (Lizards tail). The drawing shows a stem with large, heart-shaped leaves. A long, slender, and hairy seed pod (silicle) is shown extending from the stem.

Septis Capensis p. 162.

Botanical illustration of Septis Capensis. The drawing shows a stem with a terminal umbel of flowers. At the base of the stem is a trifoliate leaf and a fibrous root system.

Legwood tree. p. 183.

Dionaea Muscipula.
Venus' Fly-trap. p. 183.

Botanical illustration of Dionaea Muscipula, showing the plant's structure with a central stem, leaves, and a flower, along with a magnified view of the trap mechanism.A botanical illustration of a Venus' Fly-trap. The main part of the image shows the plant's structure, including a central stem, several large, heart-shaped leaves with ciliate margins, and a flower at the top. To the left, there is a magnified view of the trap mechanism, showing the hinged lid and the sticky, glandular hairs on the inner surface of the trap.

p. 186.

Botanical illustration of Cherleria sedoides, showing a dense, bushy plant with fine, needle-like leaves.A botanical illustration of a dense, bushy plant. The leaves are very fine, needle-like, and arranged in a whorled pattern along the stems, giving the plant a feathery appearance.

p. 186.

Camellia Alta.
White Cinnamon p. 190.

Botanical illustration of Camellia Alta (White Cinnamon).A detailed botanical engraving of a Camellia Alta branch. The branch features several large, ovate leaves with prominent midribs and serrated margins. At the tips of the branches are clusters of small, round, dark berries. The central stem is straight and divides into several smaller branches bearing leaves and fruit.

Halesia Snowdrop Tree p. 190.

Botanical illustration of Halesia Snowdrop Tree.A botanical engraving of a Halesia branch. It shows a slender stem with several small, lanceolate leaves. Hanging from the stem are numerous small, bell-shaped flowers, each with a distinct calyx and a long, drooping style. The flowers are shown in various stages of development, from buds to fully opened flowers.

Asarum Europaeum p. 190.

Botanical illustration of Asarum Europaeum.A botanical engraving of an Asarum Europaeum branch. It features two large, heart-shaped (cordate) leaves with deeply lobed margins and prominent venation. Below the leaves, a small, developing fruit is shown, which is a small, rounded structure with a pointed tip and a persistent style.

Woll Prin. Wal. Sulphur. p. 190.

Plate CX.

Botanical illustration of the Mangosteen Tree (Garcinia Mangostana). The central image shows a branch with large, ovate leaves and a single flower at the top. ROSE

ROSE

ROSE

ROSE

Botanical illustration of a rose branch with leaves, a flower bud, and a cross-section of a rose hip.A botanical illustration of a rose branch. The central stem is vertical and has several pairs of leaves. The leaves are ovate with serrated margins and prominent pinnate venation. At the top of the stem, there is a small, unopened flower bud. On the left side of the stem, there is a cluster of three rose hips, which are the mature, fleshy receptacles of the rose flower. On the right side, there is a single rose hip. At the bottom of the stem, there is a large, circular cross-section of a rose hip, showing the internal structure with seeds and the outer layers.

Myrtus Pimento.
Jamaica Pepper Tree. p. 104.

Illustration of fruit.A small, separate botanical illustration showing a cluster of four small, round fruits on a short stem. The label 'Fruit.' is positioned above this cluster.

Dryas octopetala. p. 199.

Botanical illustration of Dryas octopetala.A botanical illustration of Dryas octopetala. It shows a plant with a cluster of deeply lobed, ovate leaves at the base. Two flowers are shown in detail, each with eight petals and a central cluster of stamens. A single, long, slender stem extends upwards, ending in a dense, feathery cluster of stamens.

Tormentilla Erecta.
Upright Tormentil. p. 199.

Botanical illustration of Tormentilla Erecta.A botanical illustration of Tormentilla Erecta. It depicts a plant with a thick, horizontal, tuberous root system. The stems are upright and branched, bearing several large, deeply palmately lobed leaves. Small, five-petaled flowers are shown at the tips of the stems. The illustration is detailed, showing the texture of the leaves and the structure of the root.

Abbott Print. Wats. Sculptor. Lond.

Faint botanical illustration of a plant with large, ovate leaves and clusters of small, round fruits or flowers.A faint botanical illustration of a plant, possibly a species of Prunus or similar. The central feature is a vertical stem with several large, ovate leaves that have rounded tips and serrated margins. At the base of the stem, there are clusters of small, round fruits or flowers. To the right of the main stem, there is a smaller, more delicate branch with several small, bell-shaped flowers hanging from it. The entire illustration is rendered in a very light, muted color, making it difficult to discern fine details. The background is a plain, off-white or light beige color, showing some signs of age and wear.
page 367 · 192174947.3.jpg

BOTANY.
Polyandria.

Plate CXIII.

Wintera Aromatica.
Winter's Bark. p. 208.

Botanical illustration of Wintera Aromatica (Winter's Bark) showing a branch with leaves and flower buds.A detailed botanical engraving of a branch of Wintera Aromatica. The branch is woody and has several large, ovate leaves with prominent veins. At the tips of the branches are clusters of small, rounded flower buds. The illustration is rendered in a classic 18th-century style with fine line work and cross-hatching for shading.
Botanical illustration showing various parts of the plant, labeled with numbers 1 through 12.A series of small, detailed botanical drawings of the plant's reproductive and vegetative parts, arranged in two rows. The top row includes: 12. a single stamen; 11. a pistil; 10. a pistil; 9. a pistil; 8. a pistil; 7. a pistil; 6. a pistil; 5. a pistil; 4. a pistil. The bottom row includes: 3. a pistil; 2. a pistil; 1. a pistil; 1. a pistil; 1. a pistil; 1. a pistil; 1. a pistil. The drawings show the internal structure of the flowers, including stamens and pistils.

Abbot Prim. Walp. sculptor fecit.

100

100

Botanical illustration of a plant with large, ovate leaves and a central stem.A botanical illustration of a plant, possibly a species of Magnolia or similar, featuring a central stem with several large, ovate leaves. The leaves have prominent veins and serrated margins. The illustration is rendered in a muted, monochromatic style, likely a woodcut or engraving. The plant is shown in a full, bushy habit, with the leaves arranged in opposite pairs along the stem.

100

page 369 · 192174973.3.jpg

BOTANY.
Didynamia.

Plate CXIV.

Ajuga Reptans.
Creeping Bugle. p. 215.

Botanical illustration of Ajuga Reptans (Creeping Bugle).A detailed botanical engraving of Ajuga Reptans. The plant is shown with a creeping, horizontal root system. A single upright stem rises from the center, bearing several opposite, ovate leaves with serrated margins. At the top of the stem is a dense, terminal raceme of small, tubular flowers.

Perilla Ceymoides. p. 217.

Botanical illustration of Perilla Ceymoides.A botanical engraving of Perilla Ceymoides. The plant features large, ovate leaves with prominent serrated margins. A tall, slender, upright stem rises from the base, densely covered with small, tubular flowers. The leaves are shown in a spread-out arrangement, and the stem is shown with several smaller, developing flower clusters.

Glechoma Hederacea.
Ground Ivy. p. 217.

Botanical illustration of Glechoma Hederacea (Ground Ivy).A botanical engraving of Glechoma Hederacea. The plant is shown with a creeping, horizontal root system. A single upright stem rises from the center, bearing several opposite, ovate leaves with serrated margins. At the top of the stem is a dense, terminal raceme of small, tubular flowers.

Lamium Album.
White Dead Nettle. p. 217.

Botanical illustration of Lamium Album (White Dead Nettle).A botanical engraving of Lamium Album. The plant is shown with a single upright stem bearing several opposite, ovate leaves with serrated margins. At the top of the stem is a dense, terminal raceme of small, tubular flowers. A detailed view of a single flower is shown at the bottom right, revealing its structure.

Abott, Prins, Wal, & Schultze, fecit.

page 370 · 192174986.3.jpg
Botanical illustration of a plant with large, broad, ovate leaves and a tall, slender, spike-like inflorescence.

This botanical illustration depicts a plant with large, broad, ovate leaves that have slightly serrated margins. The leaves are arranged alternately along a central stem. At the top of the plant is a tall, slender, spike-like inflorescence with numerous small, densely packed flowers or fruits. The plant is shown with its root system at the base. The illustration is rendered in a muted, greenish-grey color palette, characteristic of older botanical prints.

Botanical illustration of a plant with smaller, more rounded leaves and a dense, branched inflorescence.

This botanical illustration shows a plant with smaller, more rounded, and somewhat heart-shaped leaves. The leaves are arranged in a more compact, branched structure compared to the first plant. The inflorescence is also branched and appears to be composed of many small, rounded structures, possibly fruits or flowers. The plant is shown with its root system at the base. The illustration is rendered in a muted, greenish-grey color palette, consistent with the first illustration.

page 371 · 192174999.3.jpg

Myagrum Perfoliatum.
Gold of Pleasure, p. 223.

BOTANY,
Tetradynamia.

Plate CXV.

Hakile Maritima.
Sea Rocket, p. 223.

Botanical illustration of Myagrum Perfoliatum (Gold of Pleasure).A detailed botanical drawing of a plant branch. The stem is slender and has several large, ovate leaves with prominent veins. At the top of the stem, there are several clusters of small, five-petaled flowers arranged in a whorl-like pattern.

Vella Annua.
Spanish Cress, p. 223.

Anastatica Microchanta.
Rose of Jericho, p. 223.

Botanical illustration of Hakile Maritima (Sea Rocket).A botanical drawing of a plant branch. The stem is relatively thick and has several small, narrow, lanceolate leaves. At the top, there are several clusters of small, tubular flowers.
Botanical illustration of Vella Annua (Spanish Cress).A botanical drawing of a plant branch. The stem is slender and has several small, narrow, lanceolate leaves. At the top, there are several clusters of small, tubular flowers.
Botanical illustration of Anastatica Microchanta (Rose of Jericho).A botanical drawing of a plant branch. The stem is slender and has several large, ovate leaves with prominent veins. At the top, there are several clusters of small, five-petaled flowers. The root system is also shown, with several roots branching out from the base of the stem.

Abbott, Prin. and, Taylor, fecit.

page 372 · 192175012.3.jpg
Botanical illustration of a plant with finely divided, fern-like leaves and small flowers.A botanical illustration of a plant with a slender, upright stem. The leaves are finely divided into small, linear segments, resembling fern fronds. Small, light-colored flowers are clustered at the tips of the branches. The drawing is rendered in a light, muted greenish-grey ink.
Botanical illustration of a plant with large, broad leaves and small flowers.A botanical illustration of a plant with a single, thick, upright stem. It features several large, broad, ovate leaves with prominent veins. Small, light-colored flowers are scattered along the upper part of the stem. The illustration is done in a light, muted greenish-grey ink.
Botanical illustration of a plant with large, rounded leaves and a thick, bulbous root.A botanical illustration of a plant with a thick, bulbous, rounded root. The leaves are large, ovate, and arranged in opposite pairs along a central stem. The leaves have a slightly wavy margin. Small, light-colored flowers are visible at the top of the stem. The drawing is rendered in a light, muted greenish-grey ink.
Botanical illustration of a plant with finely divided, fern-like leaves and small flowers.A botanical illustration of a plant with a slender, upright stem. The leaves are finely divided into small, linear segments, similar to the first plant. Small, light-colored flowers are clustered at the tips of the branches. The drawing is rendered in a light, muted greenish-grey ink.
page 373 · 192175025.3.jpg

BOTANY.
Monadelphia.

Plate CXVI.

Terraria Pavonia, p. 231.

Botanical illustration of Terraria Pavonia, showing a flower with five spotted petals and a central cluster of stamens, attached to a stem with leaves.A detailed botanical engraving of a flower from the genus Pavonia. The flower has five petals, each marked with a dark, irregular spot. The center of the flower is filled with numerous stamens. The flower is shown on a stem with several lanceolate leaves.

Aionia Capensis, p. 232.

Botanical illustration of Aionia Capensis, showing a flowering branch with several tubular flowers and narrow leaves.An engraving of a flowering branch from the genus Aionia. The branch is slender and bears several narrow, lanceolate leaves. At the top of the branch, there are two tubular flowers with prominent, protruding stamens.

Bombax Heptaphyllum.
Silk Cotton tree, p. 233.

Botanical illustration of Bombax Heptaphyllum, showing a branch with a large, palmately compound leaf and a cluster of small, tubular flowers.An engraving of a branch from the Silk Cotton tree. The branch features a large, palmately compound leaf with seven leaflets. A smaller branch from the same plant is shown, bearing a cluster of small, tubular flowers.

Adansonia Digitata.
Sour Gourd, p. 233.

Botanical illustration of Adansonia Digitata, showing a large, flat, lobed leaf and a long, curved stem with a terminal flower.An engraving of a large, flat, lobed leaf from the Sour Gourd tree. The leaf has several broad, rounded lobes. A long, curved stem rises from the center of the leaf, terminating in a small flower.

Atoll Paris, Walp. sculptor fecit.

page 374 · 192175038.3.jpg
Faint botanical illustration of four plants on aged paper.A faint botanical illustration on aged, yellowish paper. The illustration depicts four distinct plant specimens. In the upper left, a tall, slender stem is shown with several small, lanceolate leaves. In the upper right, a plant with a more complex structure is visible, featuring a cluster of small flowers or buds at the top and several larger, rounded leaves. In the lower left, a large, broad, rounded leaf is shown, possibly a single leaf or a small cluster. In the lower right, a plant with a central stem and several large, pointed leaves is depicted. The entire illustration is rendered in a very light, muted green or greyish-green ink, making it appear almost like a watermark or a faded print. The paper itself shows signs of age, with some minor discoloration and small spots.
page 375 · 192175051.3.jpg

Monnieria Trifolia. p. 237.

BOTANY.
Diadelphia.

Plate C XVII.

Geoffroya Spinosa. Base Cabbage tree.
p. 240.

Botanical illustration of four plants: Monnieria Trifolia, Geoffroya Spinosa, Arachis hypogaea, and Bisferula Polecina.A detailed botanical engraving featuring four distinct plant specimens. The central figure is a large branch of Monnieria Trifolia, characterized by its bipinnate leaves and a dense, terminal raceme of small flowers. To the upper right, a single, smooth, oval-shaped fruit of Geoffroya Spinosa is depicted. In the lower left corner, a smaller branch of Arachis hypogaea is shown, featuring trifoliate leaves and small, round, developing pods. In the lower right corner, a branch of Bisferula Polecina is illustrated, showing its serrated leaves and a terminal raceme of small flowers. The entire illustration is rendered in a classic black and white engraving style with fine line work and cross-hatching for texture and shading.

Arachis Hypogaea.
Groundnut.
p. 238.

Bisferula Polecina.
Base Hutchet Vetch.
p. 240.

Adell. Prin. Wal. sculptor. fecit.

A faint botanical illustration of a plant with large, lobed leaves and a cluster of small, round fruits or flowers, possibly a species of Eucalyptus or similar.A faint botanical illustration of a plant, possibly a species of Eucalyptus or similar. The plant features large, lobed leaves and a central cluster of small, round fruits or flowers. The illustration is rendered in a light, muted greenish-grey tone, appearing as a watermark or a faded print on the aged, yellowish paper. The background shows signs of age, including small brown spots and a faint horizontal line near the top.
page 377 · 192175077.3.jpg

BOTANY.
Polyadelphia.

Plate CXVIII.

Citrus Medica. Forbidden fruit tree. p. 242.

Botanical illustration of Citrus Medica (Forbidden fruit tree).A detailed botanical engraving of a Citrus Medica tree. The tree has a central trunk that branches out into several smaller limbs. The leaves are small, oval-shaped, and arranged in opposite pairs along the branches. Small clusters of flowers are visible at the tips of some branches. The tree is shown growing from a small mound of earth.

Monsonia speciosa.
p. 242.

Theobroma Cacao.
Cacao or Chocolate nut tree.
p. 242.

Botanical illustration of Theobroma Cacao (Cacao or Chocolate nut tree).A detailed botanical engraving of a Theobroma Cacao tree. The illustration shows a thick, textured trunk with a large, ribbed, oval-shaped fruit hanging from a branch. The fruit has a distinct pattern of small, raised bumps. Next to the trunk is a large, single leaf with prominent veins and a serrated margin. Small flowers are also depicted on the branches.
Botanical illustration of Monsonia speciosa.A detailed botanical engraving of Monsonia speciosa. The illustration shows a plant with several large, heart-shaped leaves with serrated edges. A long, slender stem rises from the leaves, bearing a cluster of flowers at the top. The flowers have five distinct petals and a long, thin style.

Walt Peirce, Watercolorist.

Botanical illustration of a tree and flowering plants.A botanical illustration featuring a central tree with a slender trunk and a dense canopy of small, oval-shaped leaves. To the right of the tree is a large, elongated, oval-shaped object, possibly a seed pod or a fruit, with a textured surface. The entire illustration is rendered in a light, muted greenish-grey tone on a cream-colored background. Artocarpus Incisa.

Botanical illustration of Artocarpus Incisa (Breadfruit Tree) and other plants. A detailed botanical engraving. Dioecia.
Plate CXXII.

Carica Papaya, Pajaro tree, Male, p. 274.

Ratis Maritima, p. 276.

Viscum Album, White Mistletoe, p. 276.

Botanical illustration of three plants: Carica Papaya, Ratis Maritima, and Viscum Album. A detailed botanical engraving showing three distinct plants. In the center is a tall, slender tree with a thick trunk and large, deeply lobed leaves, identified as a male Papaya tree. To the left is a smaller plant with a vertical stem and small, oval leaves, identified as Ratis Maritima. To the right is a branch of White Mistletoe with small, oval leaves and clusters of small, round fruits. The plants are rendered in fine line work with some stippling for texture and shading.

page 389 · 192175233.3.jpg
BOTANY.
Dioecia.
Plate CXXIV.
Myristica Moschata. Nutmeg tree.
Botanical illustration of Myristica Moschata (Nutmeg tree) showing various parts of the plant and fruit.

This botanical plate illustrates the Myristica Moschata, or Nutmeg tree. The central figure (a) shows a branch with several large, ovate leaves and two fruits. One fruit is whole, and the other is split open to reveal the internal structure. To the right, a detailed view (b) shows a cross-section of a fruit, revealing the internal chambers and the central nutmeg. Below this, several smaller illustrations show different parts of the fruit: (c) a single nutmeg, (d) a cross-section of a nutmeg, (e) a longitudinal section of a nutmeg, (f) a cross-section of a nutmeg, (g) a whole nutmeg, and (h) a whole nutmeg. To the left, a separate illustration (i) shows the 'Mace covering the Nutmeg', which is a complex, branched structure. The caption 'Different parts of the fruit.' is placed near the top right, and 'Mace covering the Nutmeg.' is placed near the bottom left. The caption 'Nutmeg.' is placed near the bottom right, and 'A Ball of mace that is the true fruit.' is placed at the bottom right.

Different parts of the fruit.
Mace covering the Nutmeg.
Nutmeg.
A Ball of mace that is the true fruit.

In the lower right corner, there is a faint, cross-shaped structure that may represent a flower or a seed pod. The overall style is that of a scientific plate or a page from a botanical manuscript.

Plate CXXVIII.

COCOS NUCIFERA, The Cocoa Nut Tree.

Botanical illustration of three palm trees: Phoenix Dactylifera (Date palm), Cocos Nucifera (Cocoa Nut Tree), and Areca Cereus (Cabbage tree).A detailed botanical engraving of three palm trees. The central tree is the tallest, with a long, slender trunk and a crown of large, feathery fronds. A cluster of small, round fruits is shown growing from the base of the fronds. To its left is a shorter tree with a more textured trunk and a dense, rounded crown of smaller fronds. To its right is a shorter tree with a more segmented trunk and a crown of long, narrow, lanceolate fronds. All three trees are shown growing from a small patch of ground with some grass.

Phoenix Dactylifera, Date palm.

Areca Cereus, Cabbage tree.

The background is a plain, off-white or light beige paper with some minor foxing or staining.

Xerophyta, 152 Xyris, 96
Xilophylla, 128 Yucca, 153

Xiphidium, 95

B O T
B O T

Botany-
Bay
||
Bothnia.

BOTANY-BAY, so called from the great number of new plants which were discovered by the naturalists who first visited it. See New Holland.

BOTANOMANCY, (from botanus, herb, and manus, divination), an ancient species of divination, by means of plants; especially sage and fig leaves. The manner of performing it was thus: the persons who consulted wrote their own names and their questions on leaves, which they exposed to the wind; and as many of the letters as remained in their own places were taken up, and being joined together, contained an answer to the question.

BOTARGO, a kind of faulage, made with the eggs and blood of the mullet, a large fish common in the Mediterranean. The best kind comes from Tunis in Barbary: It must be chosen dry and reddish. The people of Provence use a great deal of it, the common way of eating it being with olive oil and lemon juice. There is also a great consumption of it throughout the Levant.

BOTE, (Sax.), signifies a recompense, satisfaction, or amends: hence comes manbote, compensation or amends for a man slain, &c. In King Ina's laws is declared what rate was ordained for expiation of this offence, according to the quality of the person slain. From hence likewise we have our common phrase, to-bost, i. e. compensationis gratia. There are bouse-bote, plough-bote, &c. privileges to tenants in cutting of wood, &c.

BOTELESS, (fine remedio). In the charter of Hen. I. to Tho. archbishop of York, it is said, "that no judgment or sum of money, shall acquit him that commits sacrilege; but he is in English called boteless, viz. without emendation." We retain the word still in common speech: as, It is boteless to attempt such a thing; that is, it is in vain to attempt it.

BOTH, JOHN AND ANDREW, Flemish painters, and pupils of Bloemart. The union of these brothers was very singular; they were inseparable in their studies, travels, and paintings. John painted the landscape part of their pictures in the manner of Lorrain, and Andrew the figures and animals in the style of Bambocche. They both died in 1650. John's taste in landscape is elegant; his ideas are grand; his composition beautiful; and his execution rich and masterly in the highest degree. His light is not always well distributed; but his figures are excellent. It is to be regretted that we have not more of his works; for they are certainly, upon the whole, among the best landscapes we have.

BOTHNIA, a province of Sweden, at the end of

the gulf of the same name. It is divided into two parts called east and west Bothnia, the former of which belongs to Finland. West Bothnia is full of mountains; the earth is sandy, and yet a scarcity of provisions is seldom known. Cattle and game are so common, salmon and a sort of herrings so plenty, and the trade of skins is so gainful, that the inhabitants can command what they want from their neighbours. There are only two towns worth mentioning, viz. Tornea and Uma. The inhabitants of this province are Protestants; and are a civil well-behaved people.

BOTRYS, BOTRUS, or Bosra, in Ancient Geography, a town of Phoenicia, on the Mediterranean, built by Saturn, (a proof at least of antiquity); twelve miles to the north of Byblus, and twenty to the south of Tripolis. Now almost in ruins, and called Boteron, or Boturn, (Pofcellus). E. Long. 37. 30. N. Lat. 34. 6.

BOTT, among bone-lace weavers, a kind of round cushion of light matter placed on the knee, whereon they work or weave their lace with bobbins, &c.

BOTT, in Zoology. See Botts.

BOTTICELLI, SANDRO, or ALESSANDRO, born at Florence in 1437, learned the rudiments of painting under Filippo Lippi. He executed several pictures for Pope Sixtus IV. and others for the city of Florence: for these he received large sums of money, all of which he expended, and died at last in great distress, aged 78. He was not only a painter but a man of letters. Baldini, according to the general report, communicated to him the secret of engraving, then newly discovered by Finiguerra their townsman. The famous edition of Dante's Poem of Hell, printed at Florence by Nicholo Lorenzo della Magna, A. D. 1481, and to which, according to some authors, Botticelli undertook to write notes, was evidently intended to have been ornamented with prints, one for each canto; and these prints (as many of them as were finished) were designed, if not engraved, by Botticelli. It is remarkable, that the two first plates only were printed upon the leaves of the book, and for want of a blank space at the head of the first canto, the plate belonging to it is placed at the bottom of the page. Blank spaces are left for all the rest; that as many of them as were finished might be pasted on. Mr. Wilbraham possesses the finest copy of this book extant in any private library; and the number of prints in it amounts to nineteen. The two first, as usual, are printed on the leaves; and the other seventeen, which follow regularly, are pasted on the blank spaces; and these apparently were all that Botticelli ever executed. About the

Botrys
||
Botticelli.

page 407 · 192175467.3.jpg
B O T
[ 333 ]
B O T

Bottle, the year 1460, it is said that he engraved a set of Bottling. plates, representing the Prophets and Sibyls. Bafan tells us that he marked these plates with a monogram composed of an A and a B joined together.

BOTTLE, a small vessel proper to contain liquors, made of leather, glass, or stone. The word is formed from butellus, or botellus, used in barbarous Latin writers, for a lesser vessel of wine; being a diminutive of bota, which denoted a butt or cask of that liquor.

The ancient Jewish bottles were casks made of goats or other wild beasts skins, with the hair on the inside, well sewed and pitched together; an aperture in one of the animal's paws serving for the mouth of the vessel.

Glass-bottles are better for cyder than those of stone. Foul glass-bottles are cured by rolling sand or small shot in them; mully bottles, by boiling them. See GLASS.

Bottles are chiefly made of thick coarse glass: though there are likewise bottles of boiled leather made and sold by the cask-makers. Fine glass-bottles covered with straw or wicker, are called flasks, or bettees. The quality of the glass has been sometimes found to affect the liquor in the bottle.

Dr Percival cautions against the practice of cleaning of wine bottles with leaden shot. It frequently happens (he thinks), through inattention, that some of the little pellets are left behind; and when wine or beer is again poured into the bottles, this mineral poison will slowly dissolve, and impregnate those vinous liquors with its deleterious qualities. The sweetness which is sometimes perceived in red port wine may arise from this cause, when such an adulteration is neither designed nor suspected.—Pottash is recommended for cleansing bottles: a small quantity in the water will clean two gros.

BOTTLING, the operation of putting up liquors in bottles corked, to keep, ripen, and improve. The writers on good husbandry give divers rules concerning the bottling of beer, cyder, and the like. The virtues of Spaw, Pyrmount, Scarborough, and other waters, depend on their being well bottled and corked, otherwise they lose both their taste and smell. To preserve them, it is necessary the bottles be filled up to the mouth, that all the air may be excluded, which is the great enemy of bottled liquors. The cork is also further secured by a cement. Some improve their bottled beer, by putting crystals of tartar, and wine or malt spirits, and others, by putting sugar boiled up with the essence of some herb, and cloves, into each bottle.

Cyder requires special precautions in the bottling; being more apt to fly, and burst the bottle, than other liquors. The best way to secure them, is to have the liquor thoroughly fine before it be bottled. For want of this, some leave the bottles open a while, or open them after two or three days bottling to give them vent. If one bottle break, through fermentation, it is best to give them all vent, and cork them up again. Mean cyder is apter to break the bottles than rich. Some soak the corks in scalding water, to render them more pliant and serviceable. Another particular to be observed is, to lay the bottles so as that the liquor may always keep the cork wet and swelled. Something also depends on the place where the bottles are set, which

ought to be such as exposes them as little as possible to the alterations and impressions of the air: the ground is better for this purpose than a frame; sand better than the bare ground; and a running water, or a spring often changed, best of all.

To hasten the ripening of bottled liquors, they are sometimes set in a warm place, or even exposed to the sun, when a few days will bring them to maturity.

BOTTOM, in a general sense, denotes the lowest part of a thing, in contradistinction to the top or uppermost part.

BOTTOM, in Navigation, is used to denote as well the channel of rivers and harbours, as the body or hull of a ship. Thus, in the former sense, we say, a gravelly bottom, clayey bottom, sandy bottom, &c. and in the latter sense, a British bottom, a Dutch bottom, &c. By statute, certain commodities imported in foreign bottoms pay a duty called petty customs, over and above what they are liable to if imported in British bottoms.

BOTTOMRY, in Commerce, (a practice which originally arose from permitting the master of a ship in a foreign country to hypothecate the ship in order to raise money to refit), is in the nature of a mortgage of a ship; when the owner takes up money to enable him to carry on his voyage, and pledges the keel or bottom of the ship (pars pro toto) as a security for the repayment. In which case it is underflood, that if the ship be lost, the lender loses also his whole money; but if it return in safety, then he shall receive back his principal, and also the premium or interest agreed upon, however it may exceed the legal rate of interest. And this is allowed to be a valid contract in all trading nations, for the benefit of commerce, and by reason of the extraordinary hazard run by the lender. And in this case, the ship and tackle, if brought home, are answerable (as well as the person of the borrower) for the money lent. But if the loan is not upon the vessel, but upon the goods and merchandise, which must necessarily be sold or exchanged in the course of the voyage, then only the borrower, personally, is bound to answer the contract; who therefore, in this case, is said to take up the money at respondentia. These terms are also applied to contracts for the repayment of money borrowed, not on the ship and goods only, but on the mere hazard of the voyage itself; when a man lends a merchant 1000l. to be employed in a beneficial trade, with condition to be repaid with extraordinary interest, in case such a voyage be safely performed; which kind of agreement, is sometimes called scuus nauticum, and sometimes usura maritima. But as this gave an opening for usurious and gaming contracts, especially upon long voyages, it was enacted by the statute 19 Geo. II. c. 37, that all monies lent on bottomry, or at respondentia, on vessels bound to or from the East Indies, shall be expressly lent only upon the ship, or upon the merchandise; that the lender shall have the benefit of salvage; and that if the borrower has not on board effects to the value of the sum borrowed, he shall be responsible to the lender for so much of the principal as hath not been laid out, with legal interest and all other charges, though the ship and merchandise be totally lost.

BOTTOMY. A cross bottomy, in Heraldry, terminates

Bottom
||
Bottomy.

page 408 · 192175480.3.jpg
B O T
[ 334 ]
B O T

Bottrigaro, nates at each end in three buds, knots, or buttons, resembling, in some measure, the three-leaved grass; on which account Segoeing, in his Trefor Heraldique, terms it croix treflee. It is the badge of the order of St Maurice. See HERALDRY Plates.

BOTTRIGARO, HERCOLE, a person eminently skilled in the science of music, though not a musician by profession. He was a man of rank in Bologna; and appears, from several letters to him that have been printed, to have had the title of Count. He published several controversial pieces on the subject of music. It seems that he entertained strong prejudices in favour of the ancient music; and that he attempted, as Vincentine and others had done, to introduce the chromatic genus into practice, but with no better success than had attended the endeavours of others. He corrected Gogavino's Latin version of Ptolemy in numberless instances; and that so good a purpose, that Dr Wallis has in general conformed to it in that translation of the same author which he gave to the world many years after. He also translated into Italian Boetius de Musica, and as much of Plutarch and Macrobius as relates to music: besides this, he made annotations upon Arifoxenus, Franchinus, Spataro, Vicentino, Zarlino, and Galilei; and, in short, on almost every musical treatise he could lay his hands on, as appears by the copies which were once his own, and are now reposed in many libraries in Italy. Of Bottrigaro's works it is said, that they contain greater proofs of his learning and skill in music, than of his abilities as a writer, his style being remarkably inelegant: nevertheless, he affected the character of a poet; and there is extant a collection of poems by him, in 8vo, printed in 1557. Walther† represents him as an able mathematician, and a collector of rarities; and says that he was possessed of a cabinet, which the emperor Ferdinand II. had a great desire to purchase. He died in 1609.

BOTTs, in Zoology, a species of worms which can be produced and nourished only in the intestines of a horse. It is there alone they can enjoy the proper temperature of heat, and receive the nourishment necessary for them. See OESTRUS, ENTOMOLOGY Index.

Besides the long worms which have been observed in the bodies of horses, there are also short ones.—By these are to be understood what we call botts.

All authors, both ancient and modern, who have treated of the diseases of horses, have taken notice of these worms; but M. Vallisnieri is the first who has traced them to the last stage of their transformation, and has seen them change into a hairy kind of fly like the drone.

The flies from which these botts are produced inhabit the country, and do not come near houses, at least not near those of great towns; and therefore horses are never liable to have the worms (i. e. botts) in their bodies, if they have been kept in the house, especially in a town, during the summer and autumn.

It is in the former of these seasons, and perhaps too in the beginning of the latter, that the females of these flies apply themselves to the anus of horses, and endeavour to gain admittance, in order there to deposit their eggs, or perhaps their worms.

The precise instant of their entrance will scarce admit of an eye-witness, but by the merest chance; yet M. Vallisnieri says, that Dr Gaspari had attained this

very uncommon sight. The Doctor (he tells us) was one day looking at his mares in the field; and from being very quiet, he observed, that on a sudden they became very restless, and ran about in great agitation, prancing, plunging, and kicking, with violent motions of their tails. He concluded, that these extraordinary effects were produced by some fly buzzing about them, and endeavouring to settle upon the anus of one of them; but the fly not being able to succeed, he observed it to go off with less noise than before, towards a mare that was feeding at a distance from the rest; and now the fly taking a more effectual method to obtain its design, passed under the tail of the mare, and so made its way to the anus. Here at first it occasioned only an itching, by which the intestine was protruded with an increasing aperture of the anus; the fly taking the advantage of this, penetrated further, and secured itself in the fold of the intestine:—this effected, it was in a situation proper for laying its eggs. Soon after this, the mare became very violent, running about, prancing, and kicking, and throwing herself on the ground; in short, was not quiet, nor returned to feeding, till after a quarter of an hour.

The fly then, we see, can find means of depositing its eggs, or perhaps its worm (i. e. botts), in the fundament of the horse; which once effected, it has done all that is necessary for them. If these bott-worms are not hatched when first deposited in the horse, but are then only eggs, it will not be long before it happens, from the nutritive heat they there receive.

These bott-worms soon make their way into the intestines of the horse: they occupy such parts of this region as are to them most convenient; and sometimes (as we shall see presently) they penetrate even to the stomach. All the hazard they appear to be exposed to, is that of being carried away from the places they have fixed on by the excrement, which may seem likely to drive all before it. But nature has provided for all things; and when we shall have further described these bott-worms, it will seem that they are able to maintain their situation, and to remain in the body of the horse, as long as they please.

There is a time when these bott-worms are of themselves desirous to leave this their habitation, it being no longer convenient for them after the purposes of their growth are answered. Their transformation to a fly must be performed out of the horse's body: and accordingly, when the time of their transformation draws near, they approach towards the anus of the horse; and then leave him of their own accord, or with the excrement, with which they then suffer themselves to be carried along.

According to M. de Reaumur's observations, the bott-worms have two unequal claws, by which they are enabled to remain in the intestines of the horse in opposition to all efforts of the excrement to force them out.—These claws are a sort of anchor, differently disposed from those of common anchors, but contrived to produce the same effect. Besides these two claws, nature has given them a very great number of triangular spines or bristles, very sufficient to arm them against the coats of the intestines, and to resist the force employed to drive them towards the anus, provided the head be directed towards the stomach of the horse.

It will be asked, no doubt, if these bott-worms are not

† Mythical
Lenten.

Botts
page 409 · 192175493.3.jpg
B O T
[ 335 ]
B O U

Botwar || Bova. not dangerous to horses?—The mares which afforded M. de Reaumur, for several years, those on which he made his observations, did not appear to be less in health than those which had none; but it may sometimes happen, that they are in so great a quantity in the body of the horse as to prove fatal to him. M. Vallisnieri supposes these bott-worms to have been the cause of an epidemical disease that destroyed a great many horses about Verona and Mantua in the year 1713.—The observations communicated to him by Dr. Caspari sufficiently confirm his supposition. This gentleman, upon dissecting some horses that died of this distemper, found in their stomachs a surprising quantity of short worms; of which to give us some idea, he compares them to the kernels of a pomegranate opened: each of these, by gnawing on the coat of the stomach, had made to itself a kind of cellule therein, each of which would easily contain a grain of Indian wheat. It is easy to imagine by this means the stomach must be reduced to a wretched condition; the outer membranes were inflamed, and the inner ones ulcerated and corrupted; a very small quantity of these worms were found in the small intestines, and only a few in the larger, to which last they were found affixed, but had not corroded them. It is only perhaps when these bott-worms are in great numbers, and thereby incommodate each other in the intestines of the horse, that they make their way towards the stomach; and indeed a very few flies must be enough to overstock the inside of a horse, provided they should deposit all their eggs, and such should all be animated, M. Vallisnieri having counted 700 and odd in the body of one single fly.

When one of these bots has left the anus of the horse, it falls on the ground; and immediately seeks out for some place of safety, where it may retire, to prepare for the last stage of its transformation, by which it is to become a fly. And now by degrees the skin hardens and thickens; and at length forms a solid shell or cod, the form of which scarce differs from that of the worm. It is first of a pale red colour, which changes into chesnut; and at length, by the addition of gradual and successive shades of brown, the shell is rendered black. The worm or bott, before it passes into a nymph, is of the form of an oblong ball; it remains in this form much longer than worms of the fleas-fly kind. M. de Reaumur met with worms that retained this figure five or six days: as yet, one can perceive no traces of the legs, wings, and head of the nymph. Hence he first learned, that those bott-worms do not become nymphs immediately upon their first change; but that, in order to become flies, they must undergo one change more than caterpillars ordinarily do to become butterflies.—For the cure of horses troubled with bots, see FARRIERY INDEX.

BOTWAR, a town of Germany, in the circle of Saxia, and subject to the duke of Wirtemberg. E. Long. 9. 15. N. Lat. 49. 0.

BOTZENBURG, a town of Germany, in the duchy of Mecklenburg. It had a castle, which was destroyed by the Danes in 1202. It is seated on the Elbe, and the vessels that pass by are obliged to pay a considerable toll. E. Long. 10. 48. N. Lat. 53. 34.

BOVA, an episcopal town of Italy, in the kingdom of Naples, seated near the Apennine mountains. E. Long. 16. 15. N. Lat. 37. 15.

BOUCHAIN, a fortified town of the French Netherlands, in the province of Hainault. It is divided into two parts by the river Scheld. It was taken by the French in 1676; and by the allies under the duke of Marlborough in 1711, which was the last military achievement of that great general; but the following year it was retaken by the French. E. Long. 3. 15. N. Lat. 50. 17.

BOUCHE OF COURT, the privilege of having meat and drink at court scot-free. The word is also written bouge, bouge, and budge; it is mere French, where it signifies mouth.—The French still use the phrase, Avoir bouche à la cour; that is, to have table or diet at court. This privilege is sometimes only extended to bread, beer, and wine: it was a custom anciently in use, as well in the houses of noblemen as in the king's court. Thomas earl of Lancaster retained Sir John de Ewre, to serve him with ten men at arms in time of war, allowing them bouge of court, with livery of hay and oats, horse-shoes and nails. Sir Hugh Merrill had the same privilege for life, on condition of serving King Edward II.

BOUCHET, JOHN, a French poet and historian, flourished in the 16th century. The most considerable of his writings are the Annals of Aquitaine, and his Chapelet des Princes.

BOUDRY, a small town of Switzerland, in the province of Neuchatel, and capital of a chateaulainry of the same name. E. Long. 7. 5. N. Lat. 47. 11.

BOVEY COAL, an inflammable fossil found in England, France, Italy, Switzerland, Germany, Ireland, &c. Its colour is brown or brownish black, and of a laminar structure. It is composed of wood, penetrated by petrol or bitumen; and frequently contains pyrites, alum, and vitriol.

BOUFLERS, LEWIS FRANCIS, DUKE OF, a peer and marshal of France, and a general of distinguished reputation, was the son of Francis count of Bouflers. He was born in the year 1644, and entering early into the army, was raised in 1669 to the rank of colonel of dragoons, and in the conquest of Lorraine, served under Marshal Crequi. In the war against Holland he served under the celebrated Turenne, and frequently distinguished himself for his skill and bravery; and when that general was killed, in 1675, he commanded the rear-guard during the retreat of the French army. After performing various military services in Germany, in Flanders, and on the frontiers of Spain, he gradually rose in rank as well as in reputation. In 1690, he was created general of the army of the Moselle. In the following year, he acted as lieutenant-general, under the king in person; and while he invested Mons was wounded in an attack on that place. He conducted the bombardment of Liege, although it was defended by a superior enemy, and he forced the allied generals to abandon Luxemburg. He was entrusted with the command of the covering army, against King William, at the siege of Namur; and for this and many other important services, he was raised in 1693 to the high rank of marshal of France. In 1694, he was appointed governor of French Flanders, and of the town of Lille. By a skilful manoeuvre he threw himself into Namur, in 1695, and held out for sixty-three days, against the combined armies of the allies under King William. Having agreed to a capitulation, he was arrested prisoner of

Bouchain
||
Bouflers.

war,

page 410 · 192175506.3.jpg
B O U
[ 336 ]
B O U

Bougeant
||
Bougie.

war, because the French had not performed the stipulated terms on which the garrison had surrendered; and when he remonstrated that the garrison should have been retained rather than himself, he received a fine compliment, by being answered, that he was estimated at 10,000 men. In the conferences which were held with the earl of Portland, and which terminated in the peace of Ryfwick, he had a principal share.

During the following war, when Lille was again threatened, in 1708, with a siege by the duke of Marlborough and Prince Eugene, Bouillers was appointed to the command, and made a very obstinate resistance of four months. His magnanimity was not less remarkable than his military conduct; for when a partisan represented to him that it would not be difficult to kill Prince Eugene, he was told by the marshal, that he might expect a great reward for taking him prisoner, but the severest punishment if any thing were attempted against his life. He was rewarded and honoured by the king for his defence of Lille, as if he had been victorious. When the affairs of France were threatened with the most urgent danger, though a senior officer to Villars, he made an offer to serve under that general, and was with him at the battle of Malplaquet. Here he again displayed his military skill, by conducting the retreat, so that he lost neither cannon nor prisoners. He died at Fountainbleau in the year 1711, at the age of 68, and left the character of a true patriot, as well as of a great commander. Madame de Maintenon, said of him, "that his heart was the last part that died." His conduct was uninfluenced by private interest, and superior to court intrigue. When he was ordered to take upon him the defence of Lille, and permitted to have the choice of his lieutenants, he waited not to arrange or regulate his private affairs, or even to take leave of his family, but flew to the place, and carried with him only two officers, one taken from the Bastille, and another who had been in disgrace; preferring merit obscured in the shades of retirement, to the gaudy flutterer in the sunshine of court favour.

BOUGEANT, WILLIAM HYACINTH, a famous Jesuit, first taught humanity at Caen and Nevers, and afterwards settled at the college of Louis the Great, where he employed himself in writing several works; the principal of which were, 1. A collection of physical observations, extracted from the best authors. 2. A history of the wars and negotiations which preceded the treaty of Westphalia. 3. The female doctor, a philosophical amusement on the language of beasts, &c. He died in 1743.

BOUGH, denotes much the same with BRANCH.—Green boughs anciently made part of the decoration of altars and temples, especially on festival occasions. Oaken boughs were offered to Jupiter; those of laurel, to Apollo; of olive, to Minerva; myrtle, to Venus; ivy, to Bacchus; pine, to Pan; and cypress to Pluto. Some make them the primitive food of mankind before acorns were invented.

BOUGIE, in the French language, signifies a wax candle, and is applied to a machine which (as the wax candle formerly was) is introduced into the urethra for removing obstructions there. Monf. Daran, a French surgeon, lately boasted of his introducing them as an improvement in his art, and acquired considerable

profit by making and selling them. Scultetus, about the middle of the 17th century, used bougies in diseases of the urethra, and Monf. Daran probably took the hint from him. Different compositions have been used, and generally mercury was a part of them. Riverius made a plaster as follows: R. ol. oliv. lb. iv. cere. citrin. lb. ii. minii & ceruss. aa. lb. i. s. tereb. venet. & rez. alb. aa. 3 iii m. Whether the bougies are made up of this or any other composition, they must be of different sizes, from the bigness of a knitting needle to that of a goose quill. They are made of linen rags, spread with a proper matter, and then rolled up as follows. Having spread any quantity of the linen rag with the composition that is chosen for the purpose, cut it into slips from six to ten inches long, and from half an inch to an inch broad: then dexterously roll them on a glazed tile into the form of a wax candle: and as the end of the bougie that is to be entered first into the urethra should be somewhat smaller than the rest, it would be as well to cut the slips a little tapering. It should also be observed, that when the bougies are rolled up, that side must be outward on which the plaster is spread.

Monf. Daran, and some others, attributed the action of their bougies to the composition they made use of in forming them. Mr Sharp apprehended, that as much of their efficacy was owing to the compression they made on the affected part, as to any other principle; and Mr Aiken very justly says, As it is evident that bougies of very different compositions succeed equally well in curing the same disorders in the urethra, it is plain that they do not act by means of any peculiar qualities in their composition, but by means of some property common to them all. This must be their mechanical form and texture, therefore their mode of action must be simple compression. The efficacy of mere compression in many cases of constriction is well known, from the use of sponge tents for widening parts that are straitened by cicatrices; and admitting obstructions in the urethra to be from a constriction formed by cicatrized ulcers, or a projection of the spongy substance of the urethra into the canal, we may easily conceive, that a gentle continued elastic compression will in time overcome the disease. We may also readily account for the inferior efficacy of metallic and whalebone bougies, from their not having the property of swelling with moisture, and therefore not making so equal a compression. As to bougies procuring a discharge of matter, there is no doubt but the mechanical stimulus of a foreign body in such a tender part, though free from disease, must produce it in some degree; and that this will be varied according to the chemically irritating quality of the composition, and the irritable state of the urethra: but it seems an absurdity to apply a topic, made uniform throughout, to the whole length of a canal, with a view of producing extraordinary effects upon a particular part of it, by means of some powerful quality in the ingredients. As to that part of the bougie which was in contact with the diseased part, being particularly covered with matter; this circumstance is probably owing to the greater irritation of that part of the urethra where the disorder is, than any other.

BOUHOURS, DOMINIC, a celebrated French critic.

Bouliers.
Motherby's
Med. Diss.

page 411 · 192175519.3.jpg
B O U
[ 337 ]
B O U

Bouillon. tic, was born at Paris in 1628; and has been by some considered as a proper person to succeed Malherbe, who died about that time. He was entered into the society of Jesuits at the age of 16; and was appointed to read lectures upon polite literature in the college of Clermont at Paris, where he had studied: but he was so incessantly attacked with the headache, that he could not pursue the destined task. He afterwards undertook the education of two sons of the duke of Longueville, which he discharged with great applause. The duke had such a regard for Bouhours, that he would needs die in his arms; and the "Account of the pious and Christian death" of this great personage was the first work which Bouhours gave the public. He was sent to Dunkirk to the Popish refugees from England; and in the midst of his missionary occupations, found means to compose and publish books. Among these were, Entretiens d'Ariste et d'Eugene, or "Dialogues between Ariste and Eugeneus;" a work of a critical nature, and concerning the French language. His book was printed no less than five times at Paris, twice at Grenoble, at Lyons, at Brussels, at Amsterdam, at Leyden, &c. and embroiled him in quarrels with a great number of censors, with Menage in particular, who, however, lived in friendship with our author before and after. The fame of this piece, and the pleasure he took in reading it, recommended Bouhours so effectually to the celebrated minister Colbert, that he trusted him with the education of his son the marquis of Segnelai. He wrote afterwards several other works; the chief of which are, 1. Remarks and doubts upon the French language. 2. Dialogues upon the art of thinking well in works of genius. 3. The life of St Ignatius. 4. The art of pleasing in conversation. 5. The life of St Francis Xavier, apostle of the Indies and of Japan. This last work was translated from the French into English by Mr Dryden, and published at London in the year 1668, with a dedication prefixed to James II.'s queen.

BOUILLON, a town of France, in the duchy of the same name, and in the county of Luxemburg, with a fortified castle, which is seated on a rock that is almost inaccessible. The French took it in 1676; upon which it was given to the duke of Bouillon. This duchy is a sovereignty, independent of France; and in 1792, the king of Great Britain granted to Philip d'Auvergne, captain in the royal navy, his license to accept the succession to the said duchy, in the case of the death of the hereditary prince, only son of the reigning duke, without issue male, pursuant to a declaration of his serene highness, in 1791, "at the desire, and with the express and formal consent of the nation." Accordingly, Captain d'Auvergne has since assumed the title of prince of Bouillon. In May 1794 this town was taken by storm, by General Beaulieu, after defeating a considerable body of republicans, and given up to pillage; 1200 French were killed, and 300 taken prisoners. It is seated near the river Semois, 12 miles north of Sedan. E. Long. 5. 20. N. Lat. 49. 45.

BOUILLON, in the manège, a lump or excrescence of flesh that grows either upon or just by the frust, - inasmuch that the frust shoots out, just like a lump of flesh, and makes the horse halt; and this is called the flesh blowing upon the frust. Manège horses, that never wet

VOL. IV. Part I.

their feet, are subject to these excrescences, which make them very lame. See FRUSH.

BOVINA AFFECTION, a distemper of black cattle, caused by a worm lodging between the skin and the flesh, and perforating the same. This distemper is not mentioned by the ancient Greeks, and is but little known in Europe.

BOVINES, a small town of the Austrian Netherlands, in the province of Namur, seated on the river Maese or Meuse, in E. Long. 4. 50. N. Lat. 49. 45.

BOVINO, an episcopal town of Italy, in the Capitanata, seated at the foot of the Apennine mountains, in E. Long. 16. 15. N. Lat. 41. 17.

BOVIUM, (Itinerary); a town of the Silures, in Britain, fifteen miles to the south of Itca Silurum, or Caer-leon, in Monmouthshire: Now called Cowbridge; according to Baudrand, Bangor in Carnarvonshire.

BOULAINVILLIERS, HENRY DE, Lord of St Saife, and an eminent French writer, was descended from a very ancient and noble family, and born at St Saife in 1658. His education was among the fathers of the oratory; where he discovered from his infancy those uncommon abilities for which he was afterwards distinguished. He applied himself principally to the study of history; and his performances in this way are numerous, and considerable. He was the author of a history of the Arabians; fourteen letters upon the ancient parliaments of France; a history of France to the reign of Charles VIII.; the state of France, with historical memoirs concerning the ancient government of that monarchy, to the time of Hugh Capet, "written (says M. Montesquieu) with a simplicity and honest freedom worthy of that ancient family from which their author was descended. M. Boulainvilliers died at Paris in 1722; and after his death was published his Life of Mahomet.

BOULANGER, NICHOLAS ANTHONY, a very singular Frenchman, was born at Paris in 1722, and died there in 1759, aged only 37. During his education, he is said to have come out of the college of Beauvais almost as ignorant as he entered into it; but struggling hard against his unaptness to learn, he at length overcame it. At seventeen, he began to study mathematics and architecture; and in three or four years made such a progress, as to be useful to the baron of Thiers, whom he accompanied to the army in quality of engineer. Afterwards he had the supervision of the highways and bridges; and he executed several public works in Champagne, Burgundy, and Lorraine. The author of his life, in the Dictionnaire des Hommes célèbres, writes, that in this province a terrible spirit discovered itself in him, which he himself did not suspect before; and this was, it seems, the spirit of "thinking philosophically." In cutting through mountains, directing and changing the courses of rivers, and in breaking up and turning over the strata of the earth, he saw a multitude of different substances, which (he thought) evinced the great antiquity of it, and a long series of revolutions which it must have undergone. From the revolutions in the globe, he passed to the changes that must have happened in the manners of men, in societies, in governments, in religion; and he formed many conjectures upon all these. To be farther satisfied, he wanted to know what, in the history of ages, had been said upon these particulars; and that

U u

Botina

II

Boulanger.

page 412 · 192175532.3.jpg
B O U
[ 338 ]
B O U

Boulanger that he might be informed from the fountain head, he learned first Latin and then Greek. Not yet content, he plunged into Hebrew, Syriac, Chaldaic, and Arabic; and acquired so immense an erudition, that, if he had lived, he would have been one of the most learned men in Europe: but death, as we have observed, prematurely took him off. His works are, 1. Traité du Despotisme Oriental, 2 vols 12mo; a very bold work; but not so bold and licentious as, 2. L'Antiquité dévoilée, 3 vols 12mo. This was posthumous. 3. He furnished to the Encyclopédie the articles Deluge, Corvée, and Société. 4. He left behind him in MS. a Dictionary, which may be regarded as a concordance in ancient and modern language. As a man, he is said to have been of a sweet, calm, and engaging temper; which, however, it is very difficult to reconcile with the dark, impetuous, ardent spirit, that appears to have actuated him as a writer.

Strutt's
Diss.

BOULANGER, John, an engraver, who flourished towards the end of the last century, was a native of France. His first manner of engraving appears to have been copied, in some degree, from that of Francis de Poilly; but soon after he adopted one of his own, which, though not original, he however greatly improved: He finished the faces, hands, and all the naked parts of his figures, very neatly with dots instead of strokes, or strokes and dots. The effect is singular enough, and by no means unpleasing: only, in some few instances, he has opposed the coarse graving of his draperies, and back-ground, so violently to the neater work of the flesh, that the outline of the latter is thereby rendered hard, and the general appearance of it flat and chalky. This style of engraving has been carried to its greatest perfection in the present day, particularly in England. He did not draw the naked parts of his figures correctly, or with fine taste. His draperies are apt to be heavy, and the folds not well marked. However, his best prints possess much merit, and are deservedly held in great esteem.

BOULAY, CÉSAR EGASSE DU, in Latin Bulenus, was born at St Ellier, a village of Maine in France; and became professor of humanity at the college of Navarre, registor, rector, and historiographer of the university of Paris.
